name: Bhishma Full
description: Bhishma, also known as Pitamaha, was a pivotal figure in the Hindu epic Mahabharata. Born as Devavrata, the eighth son of King Shantanu and the river goddess Ganga, he renounced his right to the throne and vowed lifelong celibacy to ensure his father's happiness and facilitate his marriage to Satyavati. This selfless act earned him the name Bhishma, meaning 'one who has taken a terrible vow.' He was granted the boon of Ichchha Mrityu (control over his own death) by his father. Bhishma was a formidable warrior, a master strategist, and a wise counselor to generations of the Kuru dynasty. He trained the Kuru princes, including both the Pandavas and the Kauravas, in warfare and statecraft. Despite his adherence to dharma, he was bound by his vow to serve the Kuru throne, leading him to fight on the side of the Kauravas in the Kurukshetra War. He knew the Kauravas were on the wrong side of dharma, but his loyalty to the throne and his inability to break his vow put him in a moral dilemma. During the war, he fought with immense valor, decimating the Pandava army for ten days until Shikhandi, a transgender warrior, was strategically placed before him. Bhishma, bound by his oath never to attack a woman or someone who was not male, lowered his weapons and was mortally wounded by Arjuna's arrows. He lay on a bed of arrows until the winter solstice, choosing his time of death to ensure his soul's auspicious departure. His teachings and wisdom, imparted from his arrow bed, continue to be revered as valuable lessons in dharma, leadership, and self-sacrifice. Bhishma represents unwavering commitment, loyalty, and the complex interplay of duty and dharma.
subtitle: The Grandfather: Loyalty and Sacrifice

keyMyths.title: Bhishma's Vow
keyMyths.description: To secure his father's happiness, Devavrata vowed celibacy and renounced his claim to the throne. This earned him the name Bhishma and a boon of choosing his own death.

keyMyths.title: Lying on the Bed of Arrows
keyMyths.description: After being mortally wounded in the Kurukshetra War, Bhishma lay on a bed of arrows for several days. He waited for the auspicious time of Uttarayana (winter solstice) to die.

keyMyths.title: Bhishma's Teachings
keyMyths.description: While on the bed of arrows, Bhishma imparted valuable lessons on dharma, statecraft, and morality to Yudhishthira. These teachings are highly revered in Hindu philosophy.

sources.author: Vyasa
sources.description: The primary source for the life and deeds of Bhishma.
